source: Invest Like the Best — Tech and consumer growth investing (EP.149)
summary: Top-decile ROICs are more persistent than historically, driven by regulatory capture and scale advantages compounded by AI feedback loops, undermining value investing mean reversion.
summary_extended: Baker argues that top-decile return on invested capital (ROIC) is dramatically more persistent today than it was 10-15 years ago, representing a structural break from the historical mean reversion that underpins value investing. He identifies two reinforcing mechanisms: first, increased regulatory capture that entrenches incumbents and raises barriers to competitive entry; second, the dominance of scale as a competitive advantage, particularly in online businesses where AI feedback loops create self-reinforcing moats — more users generate more data, which trains better AI, which attracts more users. This structural shift, Baker contends, explains the prolonged underperformance of value investing strategies that rely on mean reversion assumptions. The investment implication is to favor high-ROIC compounders with dominant scale positions over cheap, low-ROIC businesses, as the traditional catalyst for value investing — competitive erosion of excess returns — is increasingly absent in the modern economy.
detail: Two structural forces are reducing mean reversion: (1) increased regulatory capture entrenching incumbents, and (2) scale being the dominant competitive advantage, especially online where AI feedback loops (more users → more data → better AI → more users) make dominant positions self-reinforcing. This explains why value investing has underperformed.
confidence_evidence: Baker presents this as a considered structural view with multiple supporting mechanisms; not speculative.
quote_fragment: top decile ROICs are dramatically more persistent than they were even 10 or 15 years ago
